Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. Being 29 and having been out of school for a while before coming back, I knew I needed to be disciplined. I'd block out about 20 hours across the week, usually in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and summaries from the course materials. The problem was that even with all that time, I felt like I was just going through the motions a lot of the time.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but honestly deadlines threw everything off. I'd have my nice schedule planned out, then an assignment would be due and suddenly I'm cramming or abandoning other coursework to focus on just one thing. With 19 online courses to juggle, it felt impossible to maintain any real consistency. Some weeks I'd be super diligent, others I'd barely touch certain subjects because something else was more urgent.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or if an assignment deadline was coming up. I'd get that little spike of anxiety thinking I might have missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to reassure myself I was still on track, even if I didn't actually do much productive work once I was there. The notification emails would prompt me too, though sometimes I'd just delete them without logging in if I was feeling overwhelmed.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, logging in after dinner became kind of automatic. I'd make tea, sit down, and just check the LMS like checking email. But that routine broke down when the workload got really intense and I started feeling bored with the content. Once the anxiety about falling behind kicked in, logging in felt more like a chore than a habit. It never really became natural the way I'd hoped.
Q5: The written materials and readings were definitely most useful for me since I learn best that way. I'd download PDFs and articles and really dig into them with my highlighter. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the instructor. I appreciated when there were clear, structured outlines of what we needed to know for exams, though those weren't always provided.
Q6: I barely touched the video lectures, honestly. I know I watched some, but sitting through nearly 150 minutes of video felt tedious when I could read the same content faster. The peer interaction features felt forced to me, so I did the minimum there. Some of the optional supplementary materials I just ignored completely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assessments or if it genuinely clarified something I was confused about. If I finished something and felt like I actually understood the material better, that was valuable. But so many activities felt like busywork, especially when I was juggling so many courses. At 29, I don't have patience for things that feel like they're just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With required work already piling up and my assignment completion being what it was, spending time on optional stuff felt irresponsible. Required activities at least had the pressure of grades attached, which honestly was sometimes the only thing getting me to do them. Optional things just fell off my radar entirely when stress was high.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded almost everything I could and worked from printed materials or my tablet offline. I'd make extensive handwritten notes, create my own study guides, and review those constantly. I also used external textbooks and websites to supplement what was in the course. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely, just me with my notes and a cup of coffee.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for hours from my printed notes and downloaded readings without ever logging into the LMS. I'd be at a coffee shop reviewing material, testing myself, rewriting concepts in my own words. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. Some of my deepest engagement with the material happened completely offline, which is frustrating when I think about how the data probably makes me look less engaged than I was.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a reading or a lecture slide deck once, then work from that for days or weeks. Why would I log back in to view something I already have saved? It's more efficient to have everything locally so I can access it without internet or navigate between documents easily. The LMS was basically a delivery system for materials I'd then use elsewhere.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. The workload across all 19 courses peaked simultaneously, and I just felt completely overwhelmed and, honestly, bored with a lot of the content. My anxiety was through the roof, and even though I'm generally resilient, I hit a wall. I started feeling disconnected from the material and questioning why I was even doing this. The peer support intervention helped a bit, but by then I'd already fallen behind.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from work and life made it hard to give the courses the attention they needed. Some weeks I had to choose between sleep and studying, and sometimes sleep won. The fatigue made everything feel harder, and confusion about certain topics made me want to avoid those modules entirely.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times without it sinking in because I was too anxious or tired. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I kept doing them because at least I was 'trying.' Looking back, that wasn't real engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the ones that felt confusing or overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety made me avoid them. It's like the more important something was, the scarier it felt to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. My assignment completion rate really suffered because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have a surge of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing through. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate. The problem was that deadline-driven work isn't quality work, and my exam score reflects that. I was so focused on just getting things submitted that I wasn't really learning the material deeply.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But honestly, feedback often came too late to be useful, or it was so brief that I didn't know how to improve. When I got negative feedback, it made my anxiety worse and sometimes made me want to disengage more. I needed more guidance than I got, especially given how many courses I was managing.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with 19 courses running simultaneously. Even when individual courses were well-organized, the sheer volume made it impossible to engage deeply with any of them. I would have benefited from a more staggered approach or clearer prioritization. The courses that had very rigid structures were easier to follow, but they also felt more boring and less flexible for my learning style.
Q19: Courses with clear reading lists and written materials made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on video content or synchronous sessions that conflicted with my schedule. Also, when assignment instructions were vague or confusing, I'd waste hours trying to figure out what was expected. The lack of flexibility in some course designs didn't accommodate different learning styles well.
Q20: I think that describes me to some extent. I logged in frequently and watched materials, but my assignment completion was only about half. For me, it was anxiety and perfectionism—I'd engage with the content but then freeze when it came to producing work. Fear of failure made me avoid submitting things that weren't perfect, which meant sometimes not submitting at all. High activity doesn't mean high productivity.
Q21: That could definitely happen if someone studies mostly offline like I do. You could barely log in but still be learning intensively from downloaded materials, textbooks, or study groups. The LMS only captures one slice of learning behavior. Someone might look disengaged online but be putting in serious work elsewhere. I wish my offline effort showed up somewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't show understanding or effort. They can't capture that I spent three hours with printed notes trying to understand a concept. They miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state during study, whether the time spent was actually productive. It's quantitative data about a qualitative experience.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you think about the content outside of study time, when you want to understand rather than just pass. For me, true engagement was rare this semester because I was spread too thin and too anxious. I was present but not really engaged most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety or how hard I was trying despite struggling. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes, the peer support conversations that helped my mood, or how much I actually cared about succeeding even though my grades don't reflect that. It can't show that I'm resilient and that I'll probably bounce back from this failure. The data tells a story, but it's not my whole story.
